title: Great Temple of Abu Simbel
description: Colossal rock-cut temple built by Ramses II to commemorate his victory at the Battle of Kadesh.

title: Small Temple of Abu Simbel
description: Dedicated to Queen Nefertari and the goddess Hathor, located next to the Great Temple.

title: Temple of Philae (Agilkia Island)
description: The Pearl of Egypt, a Ptolemaic temple complex dedicated to the goddess Isis.

title: Trajan's Kiosk
description: The iconic Pharaoh's Bed at Philae, a symbol of the island.

title: New Kalabsha Site
description: An island south of the High Dam hosting several relocated temples like Kalabsha and Beit el-Wali.

title: Temple of Kalabsha
description: A Roman-era temple dedicated to the Nubian god Mandulis, relocated to New Kalabsha.

title: Temple of Beit el-Wali
description: A rock-cut temple built by Ramses II, known for its vibrant reliefs depicting battles in Nubia.

title: Kiosk of Qertassi
description: A graceful Roman kiosk with Hathor-headed columns, moved to New Kalabsha.

title: Temple of Wadi es-Sebua
description: The Valley of the Lions, featuring a avenue of sphinxes and a rock-cut sanctuary of Ramses II.

title: Temple of Dakka
description: A Greco-Roman temple dedicated to Thoth of Pnubs, relocated to the Wadi es-Sebua cluster.

title: Temple of Maharraqa
description: A small unfinished Roman temple dedicated to Serapis and Isis, part of the Wadi es-Sebua site.

title: Temple of Amada
description: The oldest Egyptian temple in Nubia (18th Dynasty), famous for its well-preserved reliefs.

title: Temple of Derr
description: A rock-cut temple built by Ramses II, relocated to the Amada site.

title: Tomb of Pennut
description: The rock-cut tomb of a Viceroy of Kush from the Ramesside period, located at Amada.

title: Qasr Ibrim
description: A strategic fortress city on a cliff (now an island), the only Nubian monument remaining on its original site.

title: Elephantine Island Ruins
description: The ancient city of Abu, featuring the Temple of Khnum, Temple of Satet, and ancient settlement.

title: Nilometer of Elephantine
description: An ancient staircase structure used to measure the Nile flood levels.

title: Aswan Museum (Elephantine)
description: Located in the historic villa of Sir William Willcocks, displaying artifacts from Elephantine and Nubia.

title: Unfinished Obelisk
description: A massive obelisk abandoned in the bedrock, offering insights into ancient stone-working techniques.

title: Tombs of the Nobles (Qubbet el-Hawa)
description: Rock-cut tombs of Old and Middle Kingdom governors, overlooking the Nile.

title: Tomb of Sarenput I
description: A beautifully decorated tomb of a 12th Dynasty governor at Qubbet el-Hawa.

title: Tomb of Harkhuf
description: Famous for the biography inscribed on its facade detailing Harkhuf's expeditions to Africa.

title: Tomb of Sabni and Mekhu
description: Double tomb of a father and son from the 6th Dynasty at Qubbet el-Hawa.

title: Monastery of St. Simeon
description: A well-preserved 7th-century fortress-monastery (Deir Anba Samaan) in the desert west of Aswan.

title: Sehel Island
description: An island filled with hundreds of rock inscriptions, including the famous Famine Stela.

title: Famine Stela
description: A rock engraving on Sehel Island describing a seven-year drought during the reign of Djoser.

title: Mausoleum of Aga Khan
description: The pink granite mausoleum of Aga Khan III, built in the Fatimid style on the west bank.

title: Old Cataract Hotel
description: A historic British colonial-era hotel where Agatha Christie wrote Death on the Nile.

title: Fatimid Cemetery
description: A vast cemetery with unique mud-brick domed tombs dating to the Fatimid era.

title: Nubian Museum
description: An award-winning museum showcasing the history, art, and culture of Nubia from prehistory to present.

title: Temple of Kom Ombo
description: A unique double temple dedicated to both the crocodile god Sobek and the falcon god Haroeris.

title: Crocodile Museum
description: Located next to Kom Ombo Temple, displaying mummified crocodiles found in the area.

title: Gebel el-Silsila (West Bank)
description: An ancient sandstone quarry site with rock-cut shrines, including the Speos of Horemheb.

title: Speos of Horemheb
description: A rock-cut temple at Gebel el-Silsila dedicated to Amun, Mut, Khonsu, Sobek, Taweret, Thoth, and the Pharaoh.

title: Temple of Edfu
description: The Temple of Horus, one of the best-preserved ancient monuments in Egypt.

title: Tell Edfu
description: The archaeological mound (tell) of the ancient city of Djeba, revealing domestic and administrative life.

title: El Kab (Ancient Nekheb)
description: The ancient capital of Upper Egypt, featuring massive city walls and temples to the vulture goddess Nekhbet.

title: Tombs of El Kab
description: Rock-cut tombs of New Kingdom officials like Ahmose, son of Ebana, and Paheri.

title: Temple of Amenhotep III (El Kab)
description: A small, remote temple in the desert wadi east of El Kab, dedicated to Nekhbet and Hathor.

title: Kom el-Ahmar (Hierakonpolis)
description: The site of Ancient Nekhen, the City of the Hawk, a major predynastic center.

title: The Fort (Hierakonpolis)
description: A massive mud-brick enclosure at Hierakonpolis built by King Khasekhemwy.

title: Pyramid of El Kula
description: A small, ruined step pyramid from the 3rd Dynasty, a hidden gem near Hierakonpolis.

title: Gebel el-Silsila (East Bank)
description: Less visited east bank quarries featuring large unfinished sphinxes and stelae.

title: Aswan High Dam Monument
description: The Lotus Tower, a monument symbolizing Arab-Soviet friendship.

title: Arch of Diocletian
description: A Roman gateway at the Philae temple complex.

title: Temple of Gerf Hussein
description: Sections of this temple of Ptah (Ramses II) were saved and relocated to New Kalabsha.
